flightOverview.title: Flight Briefing
flightOverview.description: Your spacecraft is built and ready for launch. You will navigate through the asteroid belt using Hall-effect thrusters powered by solar electric propulsion. Use your engines carefully to avoid collisions and reach asteroid Psyche.

funFacts.fact: The Psyche spacecraft uses Hall-effect thrusters that ionize xenon gas and accelerate it with electromagnetic fields, producing a beautiful blue glow as exhaust.
Solar electric propulsion is incredibly efficient — the Psyche spacecraft can change its velocity by over 3.1 miles per second (5 km/s) using only about 2,030 pounds of xenon fuel.
At maximum power, the spacecraft's thrusters produce only about 240 millinewtons of force — roughly the weight of three quarters sitting in your hand.
Despite what movies show, the asteroid belt is mostly empty space. The average distance between asteroids is about 600,000 miles (1 million km).
The Psyche spacecraft uses star trackers — cameras that photograph star patterns — to determine its exact orientation in space at all times.
NASA's Deep Space Network, with antennas in California, Spain, and Australia, communicates with the Psyche spacecraft as it travels through deep space.
At its farthest point, radio signals from the Psyche spacecraft take over 25 minutes to reach Earth, traveling at the speed of light.
The Psyche spacecraft will travel roughly 2.2 billion miles (3.6 billion km) over nearly six years to reach asteroid Psyche in August 2029.
In May 2026, Psyche performed a gravity assist flyby of Mars, using the planet's gravitational pull to slingshot toward the asteroid belt and save fuel.
The spacecraft's five-panel solar arrays span 75 feet (22.9 m) tip to tip and generate over 20 kilowatts of power near Earth — enough to power several homes.
In the vacuum of space, there is no friction or air resistance. Once an object starts moving, it will continue at the same speed forever unless another force acts on it.
To slow down in space, you have to fire your engines in the opposite direction of travel. There are no brakes in the vacuum of space.
Orbital mechanics is counterintuitive: to catch up to something ahead of you in orbit, you actually need to slow down first, dropping to a lower, faster orbit.
Even a paint flake traveling at orbital speeds (17,500 mph / 28,000 km/h) can crack a spacecraft window. Micrometeorites are a constant danger in space.
Solar storms can damage spacecraft electronics and disrupt communications. The Psyche spacecraft is designed to withstand the harsh radiation environment of deep space.
Space debris in low Earth orbit travels at about 17,500 mph (28,000 km/h) — roughly 10 times faster than a bullet. Even tiny fragments can cause catastrophic damage.
The Psyche mission carries an experimental Deep Space Optical Communication (DSOC) system that uses near-infrared lasers to send data 10 to 100 times faster than radio.
The spacecraft's computer processes commands autonomously since real-time control is impossible with communication delays of up to 25 minutes each way.
Psyche's Multispectral Imager uses filters to see in wavelengths invisible to the human eye, revealing the asteroid's mineral composition from orbit.
If you could throw a baseball at 99.9% the speed of light, it would hit with the force of a nuclear bomb — but in space, even slow objects carry enormous kinetic energy over vast distances.
